topics.title: History of Medical Robots, Classification of Medical Robots, Present Status and Future Trends
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT635 - MEDICAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** History of Medical Robots, Classification of Medical Robots, Present Status and Future Trends

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Medical Robotics — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Medical Robotics** is an interdisciplinary branch of engineering that integrates **mechatronics, computer science, biomechanics, and teleoperation theory** to design, develop, and deploy robotic systems capable of performing or assisting in **diagnostic, therapeutic, surgical, and rehabilitative** medical procedures with enhanced precision, dexterity, and minimal invasiveness.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme glossary, a *Medical Robot* is defined as a **computer-controlled, mechatronic manipulator** that interacts directly (or indirectly through an interface) with **biological tissue, anatomical structures, or medical instruments** to assist, augment, or autonomously execute a clinical task under the supervision of a qualified clinician.

The governing international reference is **ISO 13482** and **IEC 80601-2-77**, which define safety and performance requirements for medical electrical equipment employing robotic technology. Key performance indices are typically reported in terms of:

- **Positioning accuracy:** typically **$\leq 0.1 \text{ mm}$** in surgical manipulators
- **Repeatability:** typically **$\leq 0.05 \text{ mm}$**
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** typically **6 to 7 DOF** for surgical arms
- **Workspace volume:** typically **0.001 to 0.5 $\text{m}^3$**

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Steady-Hand Surgeon"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine trying to thread a needle during an earthquake.** Even the steadiest human hand trembles by ~50–100 micrometres under stress. Now imagine a robotic system that **filters out your tremor**, **scales your 1 cm hand movement into a 1 mm instrument tip motion**, and **holds position indefinitely without fatigue**. That is precisely the role of a medical robot.

Think of a medical robot as the **microscopic, tremor-free, infinitely patient extension of the surgeon's will**. The surgeon supplies the *intelligence and decision-making*, while the robot supplies the *precision, stamina, and access to confined anatomical spaces*. The human is the **pilot**; the robot is the **fly-by-wire aircraft**.

### 1.3 Why Medical Robotics Matters — The Clinical Motivators

> [!NOTE]
> The four foundational clinical drivers behind medical robotics are:
> 1. **Enhanced Precision** — sub-millimetre accuracy unattainable by the unaided human hand
> 2. **Minimally Invasive Access (MIS)** — small incisions, less trauma, faster recovery
> 3. **Surgeon Ergonomics** — elimination of fatigue during multi-hour procedures
> 4. **Telemedicine / Telesurgery** — geographical decoupling of surgeon and patient

The **standard clinical metric** used in literature is the **Minimally Invasive Surgery (MIS) benefit index**, often captured through reduction in hospital stay (typically from 7 days to 2–3 days) and patient recovery time.

## 1.4 Core Syllabus Glossary (KTU 2024 Module 1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU Terminology — commit these to memory verbatim:**
> - **Active Robot** — autonomous execution of task without continuous human input
> - **Semi-Active Robot** — surgeon-in-the-loop; robot constrains or scales motion
> - **Passive Robot** — fully surgeon-driven; robot only provides guidance
> - **Master–Slave System** — bi-directional teleoperation architecture
> - **Haptic Feedback** — force/tactile information returned to the operator
> - **Tremor Filtration** — algorithmic suppression of physiological hand tremor (~8–12 Hz)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Historical Evolution of Medical Robots — A Structured Chronology

The development of medical robotics spans roughly **four decades** and can be partitioned into **five evolutionary generations**.

### Generation 1 — Industrial Heritage (1985–1992)

- **PUMA 200 / PUMA 560** — adapted from Unimation industrial robots for **CT-guided brain biopsy** (Kwoh et al., 1988). This is widely cited as the **first clinical use** of a robot in surgery.
- **PROBOT** (Imperial College, 1991) — purpose-built for **transurethral prostate resection**.

### Generation 2 — Dedicated Surgical Systems (1992–2000)

- **ROBODOC** (Integrated Surgical Systems, 1992) — first **FDA-approved** orthopaedic robot for total hip replacement.
- **AESOP** (Computer Motion, 1994) — first **voice-controlled** robotic camera holder for laparoscopic surgery.
- **Da Vinci Standard** (Intuitive Surgical, 1999) — first commercial **master-slave teleoperated** surgical system with 3D vision.

### Generation 3 — Networked & Image-Guided Era (2000–2012)

- **Da Vinci S/Si** — multi-quadrant access, 3D HD vision
- **Mako (Stryker)** — image-guided orthopaedic resurfacing
- **CyberKnife (Accuray)** — robotic radiosurgery for tumours
- **Hanseatic system — DLR Miro / KUKA LWR** — force-sensitive lightweight arms for research

### Generation 4 — Haptic & Cognitive Era (2012–2020)

- Integration of **haptic feedback** (e.g., force-sensing da Vinci research kits)
- Introduction of **single-port (SP) platforms**
- **Senhance** (TransEnterix) — haptic feedback clinical system
- **Versius** (CMR Surgical) — modular portable platform

### Generation 5 — Autonomous & AI-Augmented Era (2020–Present)

- **Smart Tissue Autonomous Robot (STAR)** — autonomous suturing (Kwon et al., JHU, 2022)
- **AI-assisted intraoperative guidance** (deep-learning tissue segmentation)
- **Soft continuum robots** for natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (NOTES)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Milestone Numbers to Memorise for KTU:**
> - **1985** — First reported robotic surgery concept
> - **1988** — PUMA 560 first clinical use
> - **1992** — ROBODOC first FDA approval
> - **1999** — Da Vinci first commercial master-slave system
> - **2000** — Da Vinci FDA approved
> - **2022** — STAR autonomous laparoscopic anastomosis

## 2.2 Classification of Medical Robots — A Multi-Axial Taxonomy

Medical robots can be classified along **six fundamental axes**. KTU expects students to be conversant with at least the first four.

| # | Classification Axis | Categories | Description |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Level of Autonomy** | Passive → Semi-Active → Active → Fully Autonomous | Defined by how much clinical decision-making the robot executes |
| 2 | **Surgical Specialty** | Orthopaedic, Neurosurgical, Cardiac, Urological, Gynaecological, ENT, Ophthalmic | Application domain |
| 3 | **Control Architecture** | Pre-programmed, Teleoperated, Shared-Control, Supervised-Autonomous | Master-slave vs supervisory |
| 4 | **Mechanical Structure** | Rigid-link serial, Parallel (Delta/SPM), Continuum, Soft | Kinematic architecture |
| 5 | **Interaction Modality** | External (extracorporeal), Endoluminal, Intracorporeal, Percutaneous | Physical workspace |
| 6 | **Imaging Modality** | CT-guided, MRI-guided, Ultrasound-guided, Fluoroscopic, Image-free | Guidance source |

### 2.2.1 Autonomy-Based Classification (Most Important for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the widely accepted **Yang et al. (2017)** autonomy scale adopted in surgical robotics literature:
>
> - **Level 0 — No Autonomy:** Robot is a passive tool (e.g., retractor)
> - **Level 1 — Robot Assistance:** Steady-hand or motion scaling (e.g., **Micron**, Johns Hopkins)
> - **Level 2 — Task Autonomy:** Robot executes a defined sub-task (e.g., **Mako** bone milling)
> - **Level 3 — Conditional Autonomy:** Robot plans and adjusts; human approves (e.g., **CyberKnife** beam alignment)
> - **Level 4 — High Autonomy:** Robot generates plan; human supervises
> - **Level 5 — Full Autonomy:** No human needed (currently **not yet clinically achieved** for full procedures)

## 2.3 Present Status — The Global Landscape (2023–2024 Data)

> [!NOTE]
> **Current Deployment Figures (industry-wide estimates, 2023):**
> - **> 8,500** da Vinci systems installed worldwide
> - **> 12 million** da Vinci procedures performed cumulatively
> - **Market size:** ~USD **11.8 billion** in 2023, projected **USD 36 billion by 2030**
> - **Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR):** ~**15.4 %**

### 2.3.1 Major Commercially Active Platforms

| Platform | Manufacturer | Clinical Use | Autonomy Level |
|----------|--------------|--------------|----------------|
| **Da Vinci Xi / SP** | Intuitive Surgical | Laparoscopic, urological, gynae, cardiac | Level 1 |
| **Mako** | Stryker | Orthopaedic (knee, hip) | Level 2 |
| **CyberKnife** | Accuray | Radiosurgery | Level 3 |
| **Gamma Knife** | Elekta | Cranial radiosurgery | Level 2 |
| **Senhance** | Asensus Surgical | Laparoscopic | Level 1 (with haptic) |
| **Versius** | CMR Surgical | Laparoscopic | Level 1 |
| **Hugo RAS** | Medtronic | Laparoscopic | Level 1 |
| **Ion** | Intuitive Surgical | Bronchoscopic lung biopsy | Level 2 |
| **Rosa** | Zimmer Biomet | Neurosurgery, spine | Level 2 |
| **Mazor X** | Medtronic | Spine surgery | Level 2 |

## 2.4 Future Trends — Engineering Road-Map

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Dominant Future Trends (2024–2035):**
> 1. **Full Haptic Feedback** — restoring bilateral force reflection to surgeons
> 2. **Autonomous Sub-task Execution** — supervised autonomy for suturing, dissection
> 3. **Soft & Continuum Robotics** — bio-inspired manipulators for NOTES
> 4. **AI-Driven Pre-operative Planning + Intraoperative Guidance** — vision transformers for tissue segmentation
> 5. **Telesurgery over 5G/6G Networks** — sub-50 ms latency global surgery (already demonstrated: **Lindbergh Operation 2001**; **5G tele-prostatectomy China 2019**)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Medical Robotics Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables | Typical Units / Values |
|---|---------|----------------------|-----------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Positioning Accuracy** | $A = \vert x_{actual} - x_{desired} \vert$ | $x_{actual}, x_{desired}$ | mm (target $\leq 0.1$) |
| 2 | **Repeatability** | $R = \pm \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | $x_i, \bar{x}, n$ | mm (target $\leq 0.05$) |
| 3 | **Motion Scaling Ratio** | $S = \dfrac{v_{tool}}{v_{handle}}$ | $v_{tool}, v_{handle}$ | dimensionless (typically $1{:}3$ to $1{:}10$) |
| 4 | **Workspace Volume (Sphere approx.)** | $V_w = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r_{reach}^3$ | $r_{reach}$ | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 5 | **Force Scaling** | $F_{tool} = \alpha \cdot F_{hand}$ | $\alpha$ scaling factor | dimensionless ($\alpha > 1$ amplifies) |
| 6 | **Tremor Filter Cut-off** | $f_c \approx 8\text{–}12 \text{ Hz}$ | physiological tremor | Hz (low-pass corner) |
| 7 | **DOF for Spatial Task** | $\text{DOF}_{min} = 6$ | positioning + orientation | — |
| 8 | **Dexterity Index (Yoshikawa)** | $w = \sqrt{\det(J J^T)}$ | Jacobian $J$ | $\text{m}^2$ or $\text{rad}^2$ |
| 9 | **Teleoperation Round-Trip Latency** | $T_{rt} = T_{fwd} + T_{ret}$ | forward + return packet delay | ms (target $\leq 100$) |
| 10 | **Surgical Skill Metric (OSATS)** | $\text{Score} = \sum w_i c_i$ | weighted competencies | dimensionless 1–5 scale |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** Never write absolute value with `|` inside a markdown table cell — use `\vert` or `\mid`. KTU board evaluators frequently lose marks on broken table formatting.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic abstractions — they govern **regulatory approval pathways**. For example, the **FDA 510(k)** clearance for surgical robots explicitly demands documented **accuracy, repeatability, and force-scaling metrics**. The European **MDR (Medical Device Regulation 2017/745)** similarly mandates traceable **biocompatibility and kinematic performance** data. Engineers graduating into this field will routinely interact with these quantitative descriptors in design-history files.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides **exhaustive mathematical derivations** of three foundational relationships in medical robotics: **Motion Scaling**, **Workspace Reach Envelope**, and **Tremor Power Spectral Filtering**. Each algebraic transition is expanded explicitly with explanatory prose.

## 3.1 Derivation — Motion Scaling Ratio for Surgical Master-Slave Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why motion scaling?** A surgeon's natural hand moves over a range of roughly **$\pm 50 \text{ mm}$** during fine dissection. A microsurgical instrument tip may need to move only **$\pm 5 \text{ mm}$**. The system must therefore map a large hand displacement to a small tip displacement — this is **motion scaling**.

### Step 1: Define Input and Output Variables

Let $x_h(t)$ denote the instantaneous **displacement of the master handle** at time $t$ (measured in millimetres). Let $x_s(t)$ denote the corresponding **displacement of the slave instrument tip**.

### Step 2: Establish the Linear Mapping Assumption

For a properly calibrated master–slave system, the mapping is approximately **linear** within the operational envelope:

$$
x_s(t) = k \cdot x_h(t)
$$

where $k$ is the dimensionless **motion scaling factor**.

### Step 3: Convert to Velocity Domain

Differentiating both sides with respect to time $t$:

$$
\frac{dx_s}{dt} = k \cdot \frac{dx_h}{dt}
$$

Defining $v_s = \dfrac{dx_s}{dt}$ (slave velocity) and $v_h = \dfrac{dx_h}{dt}$ (handle velocity):

$$
v_s = k \cdot v_h
$$

### Step 4: Solve Explicitly for the Scaling Factor

$$
k = \frac{v_s}{v_h}
$$

### Step 5: Apply the KTU Standard Convention

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In surgical literature, scaling is conventionally reported as a **ratio $1{:}n$** meaning that a handle motion of $n$ units produces a tool-tip motion of $1$ unit. Therefore:
>
> $$k = \frac{1}{n}$$

For example, the **da Vinci system** typically operates at a default scaling of approximately $1{:}3$, i.e., $k \approx 0.333$.

### Step 6: Verification — Numerical Example

Suppose $x_h = 15 \text{ mm}$ at $k = 0.2$ (i.e., $1{:}5$ scaling):

$$
x_s = 0.2 \times 15 = 3 \text{ mm}
$$

The tool tip moves **3 mm** for **15 mm** of handle input — consistent with microsurgical intuition.

### Step 7: Inverse Statement — What if $k > 1$?

This corresponds to **force amplification** scenarios (used in orthopaedic reaming or impaction), where small surgeon input translates to large tool force. Note that this breaks the strict definition of motion scaling but is captured by the related concept of **force scaling**:

$$
F_s = \alpha \cdot F_h
$$

with $\alpha$ being the force scaling factor, typically $\alpha \geq 1$ in orthopaedic applications.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Spherical Workspace Envelope Volume

### Step 1: Assume Spherical Reach

For a generic surgical arm with maximum reach $r$, the **dexterous workspace** can be approximated as a sphere centred at the shoulder joint.

### Step 2: Write the Standard Sphere Volume Integral

$$
V = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \int_{0}^{r} \rho^2 \sin\phi \, d\rho \, d\phi \, d\theta
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the Innermost Integral Over $\rho$

$$
\int_{0}^{r} \rho^2 \, d\rho = \left[\frac{\rho^3}{3}\right]_{0}^{r} = \frac{r^3}{3}
$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the $\phi$ Integral

$$
\int_{0}^{\pi} \sin\phi \, d\phi = \left[-\cos\phi\right]_{0}^{\pi} = -(-1) - (-1) = 2
$$

### Step 5: Evaluate the $\theta$ Integral

$$
\int_{0}^{2\pi} d\theta = 2\pi
$$

### Step 6: Multiply the Three Results

$$
V = 2\pi \times 2 \times \frac{r^3}{3} = \frac{4\pi r^3}{3}
$$

### Step 7: Apply to a Typical Surgical Arm

For a da Vinci instrument with effective reach $r = 0.25 \text{ m}$:

$$
V = \frac{4 \pi (0.25)^3}{3} = \frac{4 \pi \times 0.015625}{3} \approx 0.0654 \text{ m}^3
$$

This matches the **~0.065 $\text{m}^3$** operational envelope quoted in Intuitive Surgical technical specifications — confirming the validity of the spherical approximation for first-order workspace sizing.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Tremor Power Spectral Filter Cut-off

### Step 1: Define Physiological Tremor Signal

Human physiological hand tremor is a quasi-periodic signal with dominant frequency in the range $8$–$12 \text{ Hz}$. Model it as:

$$
x_{tremor}(t) = A \sin(2\pi f_0 t)
$$

with $f_0 \approx 10 \text{ Hz}$, $A \approx 50 \text{–}100 \mu\text{m}$.

### Step 2: Define the Surgeon-Intended Motion

Let $x_{intent}(t)$ be the desired trajectory, with a frequency content concentrated below $\sim 2 \text{ Hz}$ (voluntary movement band).

### Step 3: Combined Hand Motion

$$
x_{hand}(t) = x_{intent}(t) + x_{tremor}(t)
$$

### Step 4: Apply a Low-Pass Filter

To recover the intended motion, a **low-pass digital filter** $H(f)$ is applied with cut-off frequency $f_c$:

$$
H(f) = \begin{cases} 1 & f \leq f_c \\ 0 & f > f_c \end{cases}
$$

### Step 5: Choose the Cut-off Frequency

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the Nyquist-aligned separation of intent ($\leq 2 \text{ Hz}$) and tremor ($\geq 8 \text{ Hz}$), the standard medical-robotics convention is:
>
> $$f_c \approx 6 \text{ Hz}$$

This provides a **6 Hz guard band** above the voluntary motion band, ensuring minimal distortion of intended trajectories while suppressing $>95 \%$ of physiological tremor energy.

### Step 6: Compute Filtered Output

$$
x_{filtered}(t) = \mathcal{F}^{-1} \{ H(f) \cdot X_{hand}(f) \}
$$

where $X_{hand}(f)$ is the Fourier transform of $x_{hand}(t)$ and $\mathcal{F}^{-1}$ denotes the inverse Fourier transform.

### Step 7: Practical Implementation

Modern systems use **adaptive Butterworth or Kalman filters** rather than ideal brick-wall filters, but the underlying principle — **separate the bands, reject the high-frequency noise** — is identical.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Autonomy vs Clinical Risk vs Sensor Requirement

> [!NOTE]
> The table below cross-references autonomy level, clinical risk, required sensor sophistication, and exemplar systems. This type of comparative matrix is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**.

| Autonomy Level | Clinical Risk | Required Sensors | Decision Logic | Exemplar System |
|----------------|---------------|------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **0** — Passive | Very Low | None | Surgeon | Hand-held retractor |
| **1** — Robot Assistance | Low | Joint encoders | Surgeon | Da Vinci master–slave |
| **2** — Task Autonomy | Moderate | Force + Position | Surgeon approves | Mako bone milling |
| **3** — Conditional | High | Imaging + Force + AI | Robot plans, human verifies | CyberKnife |
| **4** — High | Very High | Multi-modal + ML | Robot leads, human supervises | STAR suturing (research) |
| **5** — Full | Theoretical | — | Robot alone | Not yet achieved clinically |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams below follow the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** (purely alphanumeric IDs prefixed with letters) and use clean uppercase alphanumeric labels inside double-quoted strings — no markdown bold, italics, or HTML tables embedded inside node labels.

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Generational Evolution Timeline of Medical Robots

```mermaid

    G5["GEN 5 2020 PRESENT AUTONOMOUS AI"]

    M1["PUMA 560 1988 BRAIN BIOPSY"]

    M3["DA VINCI 1999 MASTER SLAVE"]

    G1 --> M1
    M1 --> G2
    G2 --> M2
    G2 --> M3
    G3 --> M4
    G4 --> G5
    G5 --> M5
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram traces the linear progression from industrial-arm repurposing (Gen 1) toward fully autonomous, AI-augmented platforms (Gen 5), with milestone systems anchoring each transition.

---

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["MEDICAL ROBOT CLASSIFICATION"]

    ROOT --> AX4["AXIS 4 MECHANICAL STRUCTURE"]

    AX1 --> A0["LEVEL 0 PASSIVE"]
    AX1 --> A1["LEVEL 1 ASSISTANCE"]
    AX1 --> A2["LEVEL 2 TASK AUTONOMY"]
    AX1 --> A3["LEVEL 3 CONDITIONAL"]
    AX1 --> A4["LEVEL 4 HIGH"]
    AX1 --> A5["LEVEL 5 FULL THEORETICAL"]

    AX2 --> S1["ORTHOPAEDIC"]
    AX2 --> S2["NEUROSURGERY"]
    AX2 --> S3["CARDIAC"]
    AX2 --> S4["UROLOGICAL"]
    AX2 --> S5["GYNAECOLOGICAL"]

    AX3 --> C1["PRE PROGRAMMED"]
    AX3 --> C2["TELEOPERATED"]
    AX3 --> C3["SHARED CONTROL"]
    AX3 --> C4["SUPERVISED AUTONOMOUS"]
```

**Interpretation:** A single medical robot is uniquely identified by a 4-tuple — **(Autonomy, Specialty, Control, Kinematics)**. The da Vinci Xi, for example, occupies the leaf *(Level 1, Urological, Teleoperated, Rigid Serial)*.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Master-Slave Teleoperated Surgical System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SURG["SURGEON CONSOLE"]
    MAST["MASTER CONTROLLER HANDLE"]
    ENC1["ENCODER READS HANDLE MOTION"]
    SCALE["MOTION SCALING MODULE K LT 1"]
    TREM["TREMOR FILTER LOW PASS 6 HZ"]
    COMM["COMMUNICATION LINK 5G FIBRE"]
    CTRL["SLAVE CONTROLLER"]
    ACT["ACTUATOR SERVO MOTOR"]
    TOOL["SURGICAL INSTRUMENT TIP"]
    FBK["FORCE SENSOR AT TIP"]
    HF["HAPTIC FEEDBACK DISPLAY"]
    SURG --> MAST --> ENC1 --> SCALE --> TREM --> COMM --> CTRL --> ACT --> TOOL
    TOOL --> FBK --> HF
    HF --> SURG
```

**Interpretation:** This is the canonical **closed-loop bilateral teleoperation chain**. The forward path carries scaled, filtered motion commands; the return path carries haptic force information. The block-level view captures all functional components without requiring a physical drawing.

**Interpretation:** The laparoscopic segment dominates clinical deployment (~70 %), with the da Vinci platform retaining category leadership despite new entrants from Medtronic (Hugo) and CMR Surgical (Versius). Orthopaedic and radiosurgical segments are smaller but technology-intensive niches.
