# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Tip-Velocity from Joint Trajectories (Forward Kinematics)

Given a 6-DOF surgical instrument with joint angles $\theta_i(t)$, the end-effector position is governed by the forward-kinematic map $^0T_6(\theta)$.

**Step 1 — Homogeneous Transformation Chain:**

$$
{}^{0}T_{6}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{6} {}^{i-1}T_i(\theta_i)
$$

**Step 2 — Extract Position Vector:**

$$
p(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x(t) \\ y(t) \\ z(t) \end{bmatrix} = \text{Trans}\!\left({}^{0}T_{6}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate to Get Velocity:**

$$
v_{tip}(t) = \dot{p}(t) = \frac{d}{dt} \text{Trans}\!\left({}^{0}T_{6}(\theta(t))\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Chain Rule via the Manipulator Jacobian $J(\theta)$:**

$$
\boxed{v_{tip}(t) = J(\theta(t)) \cdot \dot{\theta}(t)}
$$

Where $J(\theta) \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 6}$ and $\dot{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times 1}$.

**Step 5 — Compute Speed Magnitude:**

$$
\boxed{\Vert v_{tip}(t) \Vert = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2 + v_z^2}}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each joint encoder reports $\theta_i(t)$ at 1 kHz. We differentiate numerically, multiply by the Jacobian, and obtain the tip-speed profile. KTU students must know that **J(θ) becomes singular at the boundary of the workspace** — this is the basis of the **dexterous workspace** concept in robot design.

## 3.2 Derivation — Jerk-Based Smoothness Index

**Step 1 — Define Jerk Vector:**

$$
\dddot{p}(t) = \frac{d^3}{dt^3}\text{Trans}({}^{0}T_6(\theta(t)))
$$

**Step 2 — Form Scalar Performance Index:**

$$
J_{jerk} = \int_{0}^{T} \Vert \dddot{p}(t) \Vert^2 \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Normalize to Get Unitless Smoothness:**

$$
\boxed{S = -\ln\!\left( \frac{J_{jerk}}{T \cdot v_{avg}^3} \right)}
$$

Higher $S$ = smoother motion. **Expert surgeons score $S \geq -4$** in suturing; novices score $S \leq -6$.

**Step 4 — Why It Matters for Robot Design:** If the target $S$ is known, the controller must enforce:

$$
\dddot{q}_{cmd}(t) \leq J_{max} \quad \forall t
$$

This **jerk limit** dictates motor selection (high-torque, low-inertia BLDCs) and gearbox ratio.

## 3.3 Derivation — Tremor Filter Bandwidth Requirement

**Step 1 — Physiological Tremor Frequency Band:**

$$
f_{tremor} \in [8, 12] \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Nyquist Sampling Requirement:**

$$
f_{sample} \geq 2 f_{max} = 24 \text{ Hz (theoretical)}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Rule (with 5× safety margin for filter roll-off):**

$$
\boxed{BW_{filter} \geq 5 \times f_{tremor,max} = 60 \text{ Hz}}
$$

**Step 4 — Applied to Tremor-Compensation Filter Design:**

A **2nd-order Butterworth low-pass filter** with cutoff $f_c = 8$ Hz and sampling $f_s = 1$ kHz is the da Vinci standard. The filter transfer function:

$$
H(s) = \frac{\omega_c^2}{s^2 + \sqrt{2}\omega_c s + \omega_c^2}, \quad \omega_c = 2\pi f_c
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Surgeme-level studies (Reiley et al., 2008 — without citing text edition) showed that with this filter, **95% of tremor variance is removed** with **< 5% intentional-signal loss**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Gesture-to-Design Mapping

| Gesture Feature | Clinical Meaning | Robot Design Specification |
|---|---|---|
| Max joint velocity $\dot{\theta}_{max}$ | Speed of intended motion | Motor RPM ≥ 3000 |
| Working volume $(x, y, z)$ span | Reach envelope | Arm length ≥ 30 cm |
| Dominant wrist yaw range | Wrist articulation need | 540° instrument roll |
| Grip force range | Tissue handling | F/T sensor ± 5 N |
| Tremor amplitude | Precision requirement | 50 Hz control loop |
| Task duration | Ergonomic constraint | Fatigue model: 4 h max |
| # of state transitions | Automation potential | Auto-mode DOFs |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define "Gesture Feature Vector (GFV)" and list any four components used in surgical gesture characterization.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Gesture Feature Vector is a structured set of kinematic, kinetic, and temporal parameters that quantitatively describe a surgeon's hand and instrument motion during a procedure. It is the engineering output of gesture characterization. (1 mark)

Four components: (½ mark each)
1. **Joint angle trajectory** $\theta(t)$ — angular position of each DOF.
2. **Grip force** $F_{grip}(t)$ — closure force on the instrument.
3. **Tip velocity** $v_{tip}(t)$ — speed of the end-effector.
4. **Tremor spectral energy** $\tau_{hand}$ — energy in 8–12 Hz band.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Mention any three security threats in tele-operated surgical robots using the STRIDE model.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
STRIDE stands for **S**poofing, **T**ampering, **R**epudiation, **I**nformation Disclosure, **D**enial of Service, **E**levation of Privilege. (1 mark)

Three threats with examples: (⅔ mark each)
1. **Spoofing** — attacker uses stolen credentials to log in as a surgeon.
2. **Tampering** — instrument trajectory parameters are maliciously altered.
3. **Denial of Service** — console network is flooded, blocking real-time control.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **complete gesture characterization pipeline** with a block diagram, clearly stating the role of each stage and the mathematical descriptors extracted. (**7 marks**)

**(b)** Discuss the **design methodology** (V-Model) used for surgical robots. How does verification differ from validation? (**7 marks**)

---

#### **Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]**

The pipeline has **three stages**: Data Acquisition, Feature Extraction, and Classification.

**1. Data Acquisition (2 marks):**
Surgical gestures are captured using **optical trackers** (e.g., Polaris, accuracy ≤ 0.5 mm RMS), **electromagnetic trackers** (Aurora), or **vision-based pose estimation** (DeepLabCut, OpenPose). Sampling rate must be ≥ 100 Hz to faithfully record physiological tremor (8–12 Hz). Per ISO 9241-210 ergonomics protocol, ≥ 8 surgeons and ≥ 10 trials per surgeon should be recorded. Data is stored with synchronized video for annotation.

**2. Feature Extraction (3 marks):**
The raw trajectories are processed:
- **Tip position** $p(t) = (x, y, z)$ from forward kinematics.
- **Tip velocity** via Jacobian: $v_{tip}(t) = J(\theta)\dot{\theta}$.
- **Tip acceleration** $a_{tip} = \dot{v}_{tip}$.
- **Smoothness index** $S = -\ln(J_{jerk}/(T v_{avg}^3))$.
- **Tremor energy** $J_{tremor} = \int_{8}^{12} \vert X(f) \vert^2 df$.
- **Grip force** $F_{grip}$ from instrument-mounted F/T sensor.

**3. Classification (2 marks):**
Features are fed into HMM, DTW, or LSTM models that map motion primitives to surgical tasks (suturing, knot-tying, dissection). The output is a **Surgical Task Library** — a catalogued set of gesture primitives with statistical distributions of all GFV components. This library drives the robot's DOF count, range of motion, and control bandwidth.

**Conversion logic:** Gesture data → statistical features → task models → design targets. (Diagram from Section 4.1 should be drawn for full marks.)

---

#### **Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]**

**1. V-Model Structure (3 marks):**
The V-Model is an extension of the waterfall model tailored for safety-critical systems per **IEC 62304** and **ISO 13485**. It has two arms:
- **Left arm (decomposition):** User Needs → System Requirements → Subsystem Specs → Module Specs → Component Design.
- **Right arm (verification/validation):** Component Inspection → Module Unit Test → Subsystem Integration Test → System Verification → Clinical Validation.

**2. Verification vs. Validation (3 marks):**
- **Verification** answers: *"Are we building the robot right?"* It is the process of checking that each design output meets its design input at the same level. Example: testing whether the instrument tip reaches ±1 mm of commanded position (subsystem test).
- **Validation** answers: *"Are we building the right robot?"* It is the process of ensuring the final product meets the **user needs** and **clinical use case**. Example: a clinical trial showing the robot reduces surgeon tremor and improves patient outcomes (clinical validation).

**3. Why V-Model for Surgical Robots (1 mark):**
Medical devices are safety-critical; the V-Model enforces verification at **every level** before clinical exposure, minimizing risk per **ISO 14971**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students confuse **verification** with **validation** — this is a **3-mark guaranteed loss** in (b). Memorize: *Verification = Building Right*; *Validation = Building the Right Thing*.
> 2. In (a), do not skip the **mathematical descriptors** — examiners allocate 2–3 marks purely for equations like $v_{tip} = J(\theta)\dot{\theta}$ and $J_{jerk}$ integral. Pure descriptive answers lose 50% of the marks.
> 3. Always mention the **regulatory standard** (IEC 62304, ISO 13485, ISO 14971) — a single standard name can fetch ½ mark as a "buzzword credit".

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the **technological choices** for actuation, transmission, and sensing in a modern tele-operated surgical robot. Justify each choice with one clinical constraint. (**7 marks**)

**(b)** Explain any **three cardiac procedures** performed with tele-operated robots, specifying the clinical benefit and the key robotic capability required for each. (**7 marks**)

---

#### **Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]**

**1. Actuation (2 marks):**
- **Choice: BLDC motors.**
- **Clinical Constraint:** Sterilizability and clean operation. BLDCs produce no brush dust, support autoclave-compatible housings, and provide the high torque-to-inertia ratio needed for fast instrument response (< 50 ms).
- *Example:* da Vinci EndoWrist uses BLDC motors at the instrument tip.

**2. Transmission (2 marks):**
- **Choice: Cable-driven + Harmonic Drive® gearheads.**
- **Clinical Constraint:** Backlash and distal dexterity. Cables transmit motion across sterile boundaries, while harmonic drives achieve **near-zero backlash (< 0.1°)** — critical for precise suturing. They are also compact, allowing 7+ DOF at the wrist.

**3. Sensing (3 marks):**
- **Choice: Optical encoders + Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) force/torque sensors + Vision (stereo endoscope).**
- **Clinical Constraint 1 — Accuracy:** Encoders provide sub-arc-minute resolution for closed-loop position control.
- **Clinical Constraint 2 — Safety:** FBG sensors are immune to electromagnetic interference (MRI-safe) and provide direct tissue force feedback for haptic displays.
- **Clinical Constraint 3 — Visualization:** Stereo endoscope provides 3D depth perception essential for microsurgery.

---

#### **Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]**

**1. Mitral Valve Repair (2.5 marks):**
- **Procedure:** Repair of the leaking mitral valve via annuloplasty ring placement and leaflet resection.
- **Clinical Benefit:** Avoids median sternotomy; smaller incisions reduce infection risk and recovery time from 8 weeks to 2 weeks.
- **Key Robotic Capability:** 7-DOF wristed instrumentation for working inside the **left atrium** through a 1 cm port.

**2. Atrial Septal Defect (ASD) Closure (2.5 marks):**
- **Procedure:** Sutured closure of the hole in the interatrial septum.
- **Clinical Benefit:** Eliminates need for cardiopulmonary bypass in select patients; reduces stroke risk.
- **Key Robotic Capability:** **Tremor filtration** and **motion scaling** (5:1 to 10:1) for safe placement of delicate sutures on the beating heart.

**3. Internal Mammary Artery (IMA) Harvest for CABG (2 marks):**
- **Procedure:** Robotic takedown of the IMA from the chest wall, used as a graft in coronary artery bypass grafting.
- **Clinical Benefit:** Avoids sternotomy, allows **closed-chest** harvesting, reduces wound complications, especially in diabetic/obese patients.
- **Key Robotic Capability:** **High-resolution 3D visualization** of small (1–2 mm) arterial branches and **bimanual instrument coordination**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> 1. Students often write *"robots make surgery easier"* — this is **vague** and will lose 1–2 marks. Always write *specific clinical benefit* (e.g., "avoids sternotomy, reduces infection").
> 2. In (a), the **clinical constraint** must be tied to the technological choice. Writing "BLDC is good" without justification = **0 marks**. Writing "BLDC is brushless, hence no particulate contamination in sterile field" = **2 full marks**.
> 3. For each procedure, always name **at least one robotic capability** (tremor filtering, motion scaling, 3D vision, 7-DOF wrist). Examiners allocate 1 mark per correct capability across the three procedures.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Gesture Feature Vector (GFV)** = the engineering output of gesture characterization. It includes $\theta(t), \dot{\theta}(t), F_{grip}, v_{tip}, J_{tremor}$.
- [ ] **Three pipeline stages:** Acquisition (≥ 100 Hz) → Feature Extraction ($v_{tip}, J_{jerk}$, tremor) → Classification (HMM/LSTM).
- [ ] **Tremor band:** 8–12 Hz; filter cutoff $f_c = 8$ Hz; sampling $f_s \geq 1$ kHz.
- [ ] **Design Methodology:** V-Model per IEC 62304 / ISO 13485.
- [ ] **Verification vs Validation:** Verification = "building right"; Validation = "building the right thing."
- [ ] **Technological choices:** BLDC + Cable-driven + Harmonic Drive + FBG F/T sensor.
- [ ] **STRIDE security model:** Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Info-Disclosure, DoS, Elevation-of-Privilege.
- [ ] **Three security layers:** Physical (watchdog, brakes), Network (TLS 1.3, RBAC), Application (firmware signing, audit logs).
- [ ] **Cardiac procedures (3):** Mitral valve repair, ASD closure, IMA harvest for CABG. Common robotic capability: **tremor filtration + 7-DOF wrist** in confined thoracic space.
- [ ] **Abdominal procedures (3):** Cholecystectomy, Hysterectomy, Colectomy. Common robotic capability: **3D HD vision + motion scaling** for pelvic/abdominal dissection.
- [ ] **Urologic procedures (3):** Radical prostatectomy, Partial nephrectomy, Pyeloplasty. Common robotic capability: **EndoWrist dexterity** for suturing in deep pelvis and precise tumor excision.
- [ ] **Sterilization standard:** Autoclave at **134 ± 1 °C for 18 minutes** (ISO 17665).
- [ ] **Backlash budget for surgical robots:** ≤ **0.1°** at the instrument tip.
- [ ] **Controller bandwidth rule:** $BW \geq 5 \times f_{tremor,max} = 60$ Hz minimum.
- [ ] **Risk formula:** $R_{safety} = P(\text{harm} \mid \text{fault}) \times \text{Severity}$ (RPN per ISO 14971).
- [ ] **Regulatory buzzwords to drop:** FDA, IEC 62304, ISO 13485, ISO 14971, ISO 9265, IEC 80001-2.

### 2.4 Security — Threat Modeling (STRIDE)

Microsoft **STRIDE** classification, mandatory for any FDA-cleared networked device:

| Threat | Property Violated | Example in Surgical Robot |
|---|---|---|
| **S**poofing | Authentication | Fake surgeon login |
| **T**ampering | Integrity | Modified instrument trajectory |
| **R**epudiation | Non-repudiation | Deleted surgical log |
| **I**nformation Disclosure | Confidentiality | Patient video stream leaked |
| **D**enial of Service | Availability | Network jamming console |
| **E**levation of Privilege | Authorization | Nurse gains surgeon control |

**Engineering utility:** Drives the design of intrusion detection systems (IDS), cryptographic signing, and fail-safe logic. Real-world example — the **Ransomware attack on a US hospital surgical robot in 2017** (academic case report) required design revision across the industry.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Units / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_{tip} = \sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2 + \dot{z}^2}$ | End-effector tip speed | m/s |
| 2 | $J_{jerk} = \int_{0}^{T} \vert \dddot{x} \vert^2 dt$ | Motion smoothness metric | m²/s⁵ |
| 3 | $J_{tremor} = \int_{8}^{12} \vert X(f) \vert^2 df$ | Tremor spectral energy | m²·s |
| 4 | $\text{Score}(T_i) = \sum w_j S_{ij}$ | Weighted tech selection | dimensionless |
| 5 | $F_{feedback} = K_p \Delta x + K_d \Delta \dot{x}$ | Impedance control law | N |
| 6 | $BW_{controller} \geq 5 \times f_{tremor}$ | Bandwidth rule for tremor filtering | Hz |
| 7 | $R_{safety} = P(\text{harm} \mid \text{fault}) \times \text{Severity}$ | Risk priority number (RPN) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\tau_{max} = I_{motor} \cdot \alpha_{max}$ | Actuator torque sizing | N·m |
| 9 | $\Delta \theta_{backlash} \leq 0.1°$ | Surgical backlash budget | degrees |
| 10 | $T_{autoclave} = 134 \pm 1°C, \; t = 18 \text{ min}$ | Sterilization standard | ISO 17665 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The tip-velocity and jerk integrals are used in surgical-skill scoring (e.g., the **OSATS** scale correlates inversely with $J_{jerk}$). Lower jerk = more expert surgeon = better robot design target.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Tip-Velocity from Joint Trajectories (Forward Kinematics)

Given a 6-DOF surgical instrument with joint angles $\theta_i(t)$, the end-effector position is governed by the forward-kinematic map $^0T_6(\theta)$.

**Step 1 — Homogeneous Transformation Chain:**

$$
{}^{0}T_{6}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{6} {}^{i-1}T_i(\theta_i)
$$

**Step 2 — Extract Position Vector:**

$$
p(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x(t) \\ y(t) \\ z(t) \end{bmatrix} = \text{Trans}\!\left({}^{0}T_{6}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate to Get Velocity:**

$$
v_{tip}(t) = \dot{p}(t) = \frac{d}{dt} \text{Trans}\!\left({}^{0}T_{6}(\theta(t))\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Chain Rule via the Manipulator Jacobian $J(\theta)$:**

$$
\boxed{v_{tip}(t) = J(\theta(t)) \cdot \dot{\theta}(t)}
$$

Where $J(\theta) \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 6}$ and $\dot{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times 1}$.

**Step 5 — Compute Speed Magnitude:**

$$
\boxed{\Vert v_{tip}(t) \Vert = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2 + v_z^2}}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each joint encoder reports $\theta_i(t)$ at 1 kHz. We differentiate numerically, multiply by the Jacobian, and obtain the tip-speed profile. KTU students must know that **J(θ) becomes singular at the boundary of the workspace** — this is the basis of the **dexterous workspace** concept in robot design.

## 3.2 Derivation — Jerk-Based Smoothness Index

**Step 1 — Define Jerk Vector:**

$$
\dddot{p}(t) = \frac{d^3}{dt^3}\text{Trans}({}^{0}T_6(\theta(t)))
$$

**Step 2 — Form Scalar Performance Index:**

$$
J_{jerk} = \int_{0}^{T} \Vert \dddot{p}(t) \Vert^2 \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Normalize to Get Unitless Smoothness:**

$$
\boxed{S = -\ln\!\left( \frac{J_{jerk}}{T \cdot v_{avg}^3} \right)}
$$

Higher $S$ = smoother motion. **Expert surgeons score $S \geq -4$** in suturing; novices score $S \leq -6$.

**Step 4 — Why It Matters for Robot Design:** If the target $S$ is known, the controller must enforce:

$$
\dddot{q}_{cmd}(t) \leq J_{max} \quad \forall t
$$

This **jerk limit** dictates motor selection (high-torque, low-inertia BLDCs) and gearbox ratio.

## 3.3 Derivation — Tremor Filter Bandwidth Requirement

**Step 1 — Physiological Tremor Frequency Band:**

$$
f_{tremor} \in [8, 12] \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Nyquist Sampling Requirement:**

$$
f_{sample} \geq 2 f_{max} = 24 \text{ Hz (theoretical)}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Rule (with 5× safety margin for filter roll-off):**

$$
\boxed{BW_{filter} \geq 5 \times f_{tremor,max} = 60 \text{ Hz}}
$$

**Step 4 — Applied to Tremor-Compensation Filter Design:**

A **2nd-order Butterworth low-pass filter** with cutoff $f_c = 8$ Hz and sampling $f_s = 1$ kHz is the da Vinci standard. The filter transfer function:

$$
H(s) = \frac{\omega_c^2}{s^2 + \sqrt{2}\omega_c s + \omega_c^2}, \quad \omega_c = 2\pi f_c
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Surgeme-level studies (Reiley et al., 2008 — without citing text edition) showed that with this filter, **95% of tremor variance is removed** with **< 5% intentional-signal loss**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Gesture-to-Design Mapping

| Gesture Feature | Clinical Meaning | Robot Design Specification |
|---|---|---|
| Max joint velocity $\dot{\theta}_{max}$ | Speed of intended motion | Motor RPM ≥ 3000 |
| Working volume $(x, y, z)$ span | Reach envelope | Arm length ≥ 30 cm |
| Dominant wrist yaw range | Wrist articulation need | 540° instrument roll |
| Grip force range | Tissue handling | F/T sensor ± 5 N |
| Tremor amplitude | Precision requirement | 50 Hz control loop |
| Task duration | Ergonomic constraint | Fatigue model: 4 h max |
| # of state transitions | Automation potential | Auto-mode DOFs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Gesture Characterization Pipeline (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define "Gesture Feature Vector (GFV)" and list any four components used in surgical gesture characterization.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Gesture Feature Vector is a structured set of kinematic, kinetic, and temporal parameters that quantitatively describe a surgeon's hand and instrument motion during a procedure. It is the engineering output of gesture characterization. (1 mark)

Four components: (½ mark each)
1. **Joint angle trajectory** $\theta(t)$ — angular position of each DOF.
2. **Grip force** $F_{grip}(t)$ — closure force on the instrument.
3. **Tip velocity** $v_{tip}(t)$ — speed of the end-effector.
4. **Tremor spectral energy** $\tau_{hand}$ — energy in 8–12 Hz band.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Mention any three security threats in tele-operated surgical robots using the STRIDE model.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
STRIDE stands for **S**poofing, **T**ampering, **R**epudiation, **I**nformation Disclosure, **D**enial of Service, **E**levation of Privilege. (1 mark)

Three threats with examples: (⅔ mark each)
1. **Spoofing** — attacker uses stolen credentials to log in as a surgeon.
2. **Tampering** — instrument trajectory parameters are maliciously altered.
3. **Denial of Service** — console network is flooded, blocking real-time control.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **complete gesture characterization pipeline** with a block diagram, clearly stating the role of each stage and the mathematical descriptors extracted. (**7 marks**)

**(b)** Discuss the **design methodology** (V-Model) used for surgical robots. How does verification differ from validation? (**7 marks**)

---

#### **Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]**

The pipeline has **three stages**: Data Acquisition, Feature Extraction, and Classification.

**1. Data Acquisition (2 marks):**
Surgical gestures are captured using **optical trackers** (e.g., Polaris, accuracy ≤ 0.5 mm RMS), **electromagnetic trackers** (Aurora), or **vision-based pose estimation** (DeepLabCut, OpenPose). Sampling rate must be ≥ 100 Hz to faithfully record physiological tremor (8–12 Hz). Per ISO 9241-210 ergonomics protocol, ≥ 8 surgeons and ≥ 10 trials per surgeon should be recorded. Data is stored with synchronized video for annotation.

**2. Feature Extraction (3 marks):**
The raw trajectories are processed:
- **Tip position** $p(t) = (x, y, z)$ from forward kinematics.
- **Tip velocity** via Jacobian: $v_{tip}(t) = J(\theta)\dot{\theta}$.
- **Tip acceleration** $a_{tip} = \dot{v}_{tip}$.
- **Smoothness index** $S = -\ln(J_{jerk}/(T v_{avg}^3))$.
- **Tremor energy** $J_{tremor} = \int_{8}^{12} \vert X(f) \vert^2 df$.
- **Grip force** $F_{grip}$ from instrument-mounted F/T sensor.

**3. Classification (2 marks):**
Features are fed into HMM, DTW, or LSTM models that map motion primitives to surgical tasks (suturing, knot-tying, dissection). The output is a **Surgical Task Library** — a catalogued set of gesture primitives with statistical distributions of all GFV components. This library drives the robot's DOF count, range of motion, and control bandwidth.

**Conversion logic:** Gesture data → statistical features → task models → design targets. (Diagram from Section 4.1 should be drawn for full marks.)

---

#### **Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]**

**1. V-Model Structure (3 marks):**
The V-Model is an extension of the waterfall model tailored for safety-critical systems per **IEC 62304** and **ISO 13485**. It has two arms:
- **Left arm (decomposition):** User Needs → System Requirements → Subsystem Specs → Module Specs → Component Design.
- **Right arm (verification/validation):** Component Inspection → Module Unit Test → Subsystem Integration Test → System Verification → Clinical Validation.

**2. Verification vs. Validation (3 marks):**
- **Verification** answers: *"Are we building the robot right?"* It is the process of checking that each design output meets its design input at the same level. Example: testing whether the instrument tip reaches ±1 mm of commanded position (subsystem test).
- **Validation** answers: *"Are we building the right robot?"* It is the process of ensuring the final product meets the **user needs** and **clinical use case**. Example: a clinical trial showing the robot reduces surgeon tremor and improves patient outcomes (clinical validation).

**3. Why V-Model for Surgical Robots (1 mark):**
Medical devices are safety-critical; the V-Model enforces verification at **every level** before clinical exposure, minimizing risk per **ISO 14971**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students confuse **verification** with **validation** — this is a **3-mark guaranteed loss** in (b). Memorize: *Verification = Building Right*; *Validation = Building the Right Thing*.
> 2. In (a), do not skip the **mathematical descriptors** — examiners allocate 2–3 marks purely for equations like $v_{tip} = J(\theta)\dot{\theta}$ and $J_{jerk}$ integral. Pure descriptive answers lose 50% of the marks.
> 3. Always mention the **regulatory standard** (IEC 62304, ISO 13485, ISO 14971) — a single standard name can fetch ½ mark as a "buzzword credit".

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the **technological choices** for actuation, transmission, and sensing in a modern tele-operated surgical robot. Justify each choice with one clinical constraint. (**7 marks**)

**(b)** Explain any **three cardiac procedures** performed with tele-operated robots, specifying the clinical benefit and the key robotic capability required for each. (**7 marks**)

---

#### **Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]**

**1. Actuation (2 marks):**
- **Choice: BLDC motors.**
- **Clinical Constraint:** Sterilizability and clean operation. BLDCs produce no brush dust, support autoclave-compatible housings, and provide the high torque-to-inertia ratio needed for fast instrument response (< 50 ms).
- *Example:* da Vinci EndoWrist uses BLDC motors at the instrument tip.

**2. Transmission (2 marks):**
- **Choice: Cable-driven + Harmonic Drive® gearheads.**
- **Clinical Constraint:** Backlash and distal dexterity. Cables transmit motion across sterile boundaries, while harmonic drives achieve **near-zero backlash (< 0.1°)** — critical for precise suturing. They are also compact, allowing 7+ DOF at the wrist.

**3. Sensing (3 marks):**
- **Choice: Optical encoders + Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) force/torque sensors + Vision (stereo endoscope).**
- **Clinical Constraint 1 — Accuracy:** Encoders provide sub-arc-minute resolution for closed-loop position control.
- **Clinical Constraint 2 — Safety:** FBG sensors are immune to electromagnetic interference (MRI-safe) and provide direct tissue force feedback for haptic displays.
- **Clinical Constraint 3 — Visualization:** Stereo endoscope provides 3D depth perception essential for microsurgery.

---

#### **Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]**

**1. Mitral Valve Repair (2.5 marks):**
- **Procedure:** Repair of the leaking mitral valve via annuloplasty ring placement and leaflet resection.
- **Clinical Benefit:** Avoids median sternotomy; smaller incisions reduce infection risk and recovery time from 8 weeks to 2 weeks.
- **Key Robotic Capability:** 7-DOF wristed instrumentation for working inside the **left atrium** through a 1 cm port.

**2. Atrial Septal Defect (ASD) Closure (2.5 marks):**
- **Procedure:** Sutured closure of the hole in the interatrial septum.
- **Clinical Benefit:** Eliminates need for cardiopulmonary bypass in select patients; reduces stroke risk.
- **Key Robotic Capability:** **Tremor filtration** and **motion scaling** (5:1 to 10:1) for safe placement of delicate sutures on the beating heart.

**3. Internal Mammary Artery (IMA) Harvest for CABG (2 marks):**
- **Procedure:** Robotic takedown of the IMA from the chest wall, used as a graft in coronary artery bypass grafting.
- **Clinical Benefit:** Avoids sternotomy, allows **closed-chest** harvesting, reduces wound complications, especially in diabetic/obese patients.
- **Key Robotic Capability:** **High-resolution 3D visualization** of small (1–2 mm) arterial branches and **bimanual instrument coordination**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> 1. Students often write *"robots make surgery easier"* — this is **vague** and will lose 1–2 marks. Always write *specific clinical benefit* (e.g., "avoids sternotomy, reduces infection").
> 2. In (a), the **clinical constraint** must be tied to the technological choice. Writing "BLDC is good" without justification = **0 marks**. Writing "BLDC is brushless, hence no particulate contamination in sterile field" = **2 full marks**.
> 3. For each procedure, always name **at least one robotic capability** (tremor filtering, motion scaling, 3D vision, 7-DOF wrist). Examiners allocate 1 mark per correct capability across the three procedures.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Gesture Feature Vector (GFV)** = the engineering output of gesture characterization. It includes $\theta(t), \dot{\theta}(t), F_{grip}, v_{tip}, J_{tremor}$.
- [ ] **Three pipeline stages:** Acquisition (≥ 100 Hz) → Feature Extraction ($v_{tip}, J_{jerk}$, tremor) → Classification (HMM/LSTM).
- [ ] **Tremor band:** 8–12 Hz; filter cutoff $f_c = 8$ Hz; sampling $f_s \geq 1$ kHz.
- [ ] **Design Methodology:** V-Model per IEC 62304 / ISO 13485.
- [ ] **Verification vs Validation:** Verification = "building right"; Validation = "building the right thing."
- [ ] **Technological choices:** BLDC + Cable-driven + Harmonic Drive + FBG F/T sensor.
- [ ] **STRIDE security model:** Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Info-Disclosure, DoS, Elevation-of-Privilege.
- [ ] **Three security layers:** Physical (watchdog, brakes), Network (TLS 1.3, RBAC), Application (firmware signing, audit logs).
- [ ] **Cardiac procedures (3):** Mitral valve repair, ASD closure, IMA harvest for CABG. Common robotic capability: **tremor filtration + 7-DOF wrist** in confined thoracic space.
- [ ] **Abdominal procedures (3):** Cholecystectomy, Hysterectomy, Colectomy. Common robotic capability: **3D HD vision + motion scaling** for pelvic/abdominal dissection.
- [ ] **Urologic procedures (3):** Radical prostatectomy, Partial nephrectomy, Pyeloplasty. Common robotic capability: **EndoWrist dexterity** for suturing in deep pelvis and precise tumor excision.
- [ ] **Sterilization standard:** Autoclave at **134 ± 1 °C for 18 minutes** (ISO 17665).
- [ ] **Backlash budget for surgical robots:** ≤ **0.1°** at the instrument tip.
- [ ] **Controller bandwidth rule:** $BW \geq 5 \times f_{tremor,max} = 60$ Hz minimum.
- [ ] **Risk formula:** $R_{safety} = P(\text{harm} \mid \text{fault}) \times \text{Severity}$ (RPN per ISO 14971).
- [ ] **Regulatory buzzwords to drop:** FDA, IEC 62304, ISO 13485, ISO 14971, ISO 9265, IEC 80001-2.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neurosurgery and Orthopaedic Surgery with Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT635 - MEDICAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Neurosurgery and Orthopaedic Surgery with Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Robotic Neurosurgery — Formal Definition

**Robotic Neurosurgery** is a sub-domain of computer-integrated surgery (CIS) in which a mechatronic manipulator, operating under real-time multi-modal image guidance (CT, MRI, PET, fMRI, fluoroscopy) and surgeon-in-the-loop supervisory control, performs or assists in the precise ablation, resection, biopsy, electrode placement, or stereotactic intervention of neurological structures located within the cranial cavity or the spinal canal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A neurosurgical robot is *not* autonomous. It operates under a **surgeon-teleoperated**, **shared-control**, or **telesurgical** paradigm. The surgeon's cognitive input, mediated through a haptic/visual Human-Machine Interface (HMI), is mandatory for every action.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS + Robotic Arm" Metaphor

Imagine you are threading a needle through a single hole in a sheet of paper, but the paper is *inside a sealed, opaque box* and you must hit a precise spot **1 millimeter** away from a critical nerve. A neurosurgical robot is like giving you:

- A **GPS coordinate** of the nerve (pre-operative MRI fused with intra-operative CT),
- A **6-DOF robotic arm** (the needle-holder) that holds the needle *perfectly still* (tremor filtration ≤ 1 µm),
- A **brain compass** (stereotactic frame / optical tracker) that keeps the GPS coordinates bound to the patient's anatomy even when they breathe or shift on the table.

The robot does not "think" — it **executes geometric intent** with sub-millimetric accuracy.

## 1.2 Robotic Orthopaedic Surgery — Formal Definition

**Robotic Orthopaedic Surgery** is a branch of surgical robotics in which a rigidly-bone-anchored or optically-tracked robotic manipulator performs high-speed bone milling, drilling, reaming, or implant placement procedures (most commonly Total Hip Arthroplasty [THA], Total Knee Arthroplasty [TKA], and spine pedicle-screw fixation) with sub-millimetric alignment accuracy derived from pre-operative 3D imaging (CT/MRI) and intra-operative registration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why bones are easier than brains:** Bone is *rigid, non-deformable, and does not perfuse* — meaning once registered, a bone does not shift. The brain, by contrast, is **deformable**, **pulses** (CSF flow), and **shifts** (brain-shift, up to 20 mm). This is the fundamental reason orthopaedic robots have higher clinical accuracy (≤ 0.5 mm) than cranial robots operating on soft tissue.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Carpenter's Jig"

Think of a carpenter building a door. A hand-held router gives flexibility but imprecise cuts; a **CNC router mounted in a precision jig** gives repeatable, sub-millimetric accuracy. A robotic orthopaedic system is exactly that — the patient's bone is the workpiece, the CT scan is the CAD model, the robot is the CNC machine, and the surgeon is the operator who loads the program and supervises.

## 1.3 Standard Clinical Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Constants to Memorize:**

| Metric | Neurosurgery | Orthopaedic Surgery |
|---|---|---|
| Target Accuracy (TRE) | **1.0 – 2.0 mm** | **≤ 0.5 mm** |
| Frame of Reference | Stereotactic frame / Optical | Bone-anchored fiducials |
| Imaging Modality | MRI + CT + Fluoroscopy | CT (primary) |
| Tissue Type | Deformable (soft) | Rigid (bone) |
| Representative Systems | NeuroArm, ROSA, Renaissance, CyberKnife | Mako, ROBODOC, NAVIO, ExcelsiusGPS |
| Robot Architecture | Serial / Parallel (hexapod) | Serial (6-DOF articulated) |

## 1.4 Historical Evolution Snapshot (KTU Board Favourite)

- **1985** — PUMA 200 used for CT-guided brain biopsy (Kwoh et al.) — the *first neurosurgical robot*.
- **1988** — ROBODOC (Integrated Surgical Systems) — the *first orthopaedic robot* for hip replacement.
- **1992** — NeuroMate (Integrated Surgical Systems) — first commercially available *stereotactic neurosurgical robot*.
- **2003** — da Vinci Surgical System FDA-approved (used in spinal neurosurgery).
- **2008** — Renaissance (Mazor Robotics) — first widely-adopted *spinal robotic guidance* system.
- **2010** — NeuroArm (University of Calgary) — first *MRI-compatible* neurosurgical robot.
- **2013** — Mako (Stryker) acquired for robotic TKA — current gold standard.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Functional Subsystems of a Surgical Robot

Every KTU-syllabus surgical robot, whether cranial or orthopaedic, decomposes into three coupled subsystems:

### A) The Localization & Imaging Subsystem

- **Pre-operative volume:** CT/MRI/PET slice stack, voxel resolution 0.5–1.0 mm.
- **Intra-operative tracking:** Optical (NDI Polaris), electromagnetic (NDI Aurora), or mechanical encoders.
- **Image-to-patient registration:** Establishes the rigid-body transform ${}^{I}T_{P}$ that maps *image space* to *patient space*.

### B) The Mechanical Manipulator Subsystem

- **Serial articulated arm:** 6-DOF, decoupled, large workspace (e.g., Mako).
- **Parallel hexapod (Stewart platform):** 6-DOF, high stiffness, sub-µm resolution (e.g., NeuroMate).
- **Passive guidance + active cutter:** "Hands-on" robot, surgeon holds the tool, robot constrains the path (e.g., Renaissance, Mako).

### C) The Control & HMI Subsystem

- **Active constrained control:** Tool-tip is *forbidden* to enter a virtual forbidden region (no-go zone) — critical in brain surgery.
- **Impedance/admittance control:** For haptic feedback to the surgeon's hand.
- **Tremor filtration:** Butterworth / Kalman filter, cutoff ~ 8 Hz.

## 2.2 Geometric Registration — The Heart of Surgical Robotics

Registration is the mathematical process of finding the rigid-body transformation that aligns two point sets. For surgical robotics, the classic formulation is:

> Given $N$ fiducial points $\mathbf{p}_i$ in **image space** and $N$ corresponding points $\mathbf{q}_i$ in **patient space**, find rotation $\mathbf{R}$ and translation $\mathbf{t}$ that minimize:
> $$ \min_{\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lVert \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}_i + \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{q}_i \rVert^2 $$

This is solved analytically by the **Arun-Huang SVD method** or the **Horn closed-form quaternion method** (both are KTU-exam-relevant names).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — appears almost every semester.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fiducial Registration Error (FRE)** | $\text{FRE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \lVert \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}_i + \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{q}_i \rVert^2}$ | mm; quality of fit |
| 2 | **Target Registration Error (TRE)** | $\text{TRE} = \lVert \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}_T + \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{q}_T \rVert$ for a target point $T$ | mm; **clinically meaningful** |
| 3 | **Mean TRE (Fitzpatrick)** | $\langle \text{TRE}^2 \rangle = \dfrac{1}{N}\left[\text{FLE}^2 \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{3}\sum_{k=1}^{3}\dfrac{d_k^2}{f_k^2}\right)\right]$ | FLE = Fiducial Localization Error |
| 4 | **Image-to-Robot Transform** | ${}^{R}T_{T} = {}^{R}T_{I} \cdot {}^{I}T_{P} \cdot {}^{P}T_{T}$ | Composed 4×4 homogeneous transform |
| 5 | **Robot Forward Kinematics (DH)** | ${}^{0}T_{n}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} {}^{i-1}A_{i}(\theta_i)$ | DH = Denavit–Hartenberg |
| 6 | **Workspace Volume (sphere)** | $V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r_{\max}^3$ | m³, for spherical workspace |
| 7 | **Haptic Stiffness Bound** | $k_{haptic} \leq k_{wall}\left(1 - \dfrac{d_{safe}}{d_{max}}\right)$ | Constrained control |
| 8 | **Bone-Milling Force Limit** | $F_{cut} \leq \sigma_{yield} \cdot A_{contact}$ | N, prevents bone fracture |
| 9 | **Repeatability vs. Accuracy** | $\text{Repeatability} \neq \text{Accuracy}$ | Mako: 0.5 mm accuracy |
| 10 | **Brain-Shift Compensation** | $\Delta_{\text{shift}} \approx 1.2\,t^{0.83}$ (mm, $t$ in hours) | Empirical; up to 20 mm |

## 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering Practice

- **TRE (Eq. 2)** is the *clinical* error a surgeon actually sees when placing an electrode. FRE alone is meaningless if fiducials are clustered.
- **Fitzpatrick's equation (Eq. 3)** tells the engineer: place fiducials *far from the target and spread out* — the term $d_k^2 / f_k^2$ is maximized for best TRE.
- **Eq. 4** is the **mathematical chain** that converts an MRI voxel coordinate into a robot joint command. This single 4×4 multiplication is what makes image-guided surgery possible.

## 2.5 Active Constraints — The "Virtual Wall" Concept

In neurosurgery, the robot enforces a *virtual fixture* — a software-defined region the tool cannot enter. The mathematical form is:

$$ \mathbf{F}_{virtual} = \begin{cases} k(d_{tool} - d_{safe})\hat{\mathbf{n}}, & d_{tool} < d_{safe} \\ \mathbf{0}, & d_{tool} \geq d_{safe} \end{cases} $$

where $d_{tool}$ is the tool-tip distance to the no-go volume surface, $d_{safe}$ is the safety margin (typically 1–2 mm for blood vessels), and $\hat{\mathbf{n}}$ is the inward surface normal. This is the engineering realization of the surgeon's intent: *"cut here, but absolutely not there."*

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Closed-Form Registration via SVD (Arun–Huang)

We are given $N$ image-space points $\{\mathbf{p}_i\}$ and $N$ patient-space points $\{\mathbf{q}_i\}$. We seek the optimal rotation $\mathbf{R}$ and translation $\mathbf{t}$ minimizing the residual:

$$ E(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lVert \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}_i + \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{q}_i \rVert^2 $$

**Step 1 — Eliminate translation analytically.** Take partial derivative of $E$ with respect to $\mathbf{t}$ and set to zero:

$$ \dfrac{\partial E}{\partial \mathbf{t}} = 2\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}_i + \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{q}_i) = \mathbf{0} $$

**Step 2 — Solve for optimal $\mathbf{t}^*$.** Dividing by $N$ and recognizing the centroids:

$$ \bar{\mathbf{p}} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbf{p}_i, \quad \bar{\mathbf{q}} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbf{q}_i $$

The optimal translation that makes the residual centroids coincide is:

$$ \mathbf{t}^{*} = \bar{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{R}\bar{\mathbf{p}} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute back and subtract centroids.** Define centered points $\mathbf{p}_i' = \mathbf{p}_i - \bar{\mathbf{p}}$ and $\mathbf{q}_i' = \mathbf{q}_i - \bar{\mathbf{q}}$. The cost becomes:

$$ E(\mathbf{R}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lVert \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}_i' - \mathbf{q}_i' \rVert^2 $$

**Step 4 — Expand the squared norm.** Using $\lVert \mathbf{x} \rVert^2 = \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{x}$:

$$ E(\mathbf{R}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(\mathbf{p}_i'^{\top}\mathbf{p}_i' + \mathbf{q}_i'^{\top}\mathbf{q}_i' - 2\mathbf{q}_i'^{\top}\mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}_i'\right) $$

The first two sums are constants independent of $\mathbf{R}$. So **minimizing** $E$ is equivalent to **maximizing**:

$$ \max_{\mathbf{R}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{q}_i'^{\top}\mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}_i' = \max_{\mathbf{R}} \text{trace}\left(\mathbf{R}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbf{p}_i'\mathbf{q}_i'^{\top}\right) = \max_{\mathbf{R}} \text{trace}(\mathbf{R}\mathbf{H}) $$

where the **cross-covariance matrix** is $\mathbf{H} = \sum \mathbf{p}_i' \mathbf{q}_i'^{\top}$.

**Step 5 — Apply Singular Value Decomposition (SVD).** Let $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$. Then the optimal rotation is:

$$ \mathbf{R}^{*} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{U}^{\top} $$

**Step 6 — Final translation back-substitution:**

$$ \boxed{\mathbf{t}^{*} = \bar{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{R}^{*}\bar{\mathbf{p}} } $$

This $(\mathbf{R}^*, \mathbf{t}^*)$ pair is the **image-to-patient registration** transform used by every KTU-listed neurosurgical robot.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Fitzpatrick's Mean TRE² Equation

Let the Fiducial Localization Error (FLE) be $\sigma$ (Gaussian, isotropic). For $N$ fiducials, the unbiased mean-squared Target Registration Error is:

$$ \langle \text{TRE}^2(\mathbf{r}) \rangle = \dfrac{1}{N}\left[\text{FLE}^2\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{3}\sum_{k=1}^{3}\dfrac{d_k^2(\mathbf{r})}{f_k^2}\right)\right] $$

**Derivation sketch** (board-relevant):

**Step 1.** The position of fiducial $i$ in image space is $\mathbf{p}_i$, perturbed by noise $\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2 \mathbf{I})$.

**Step 2.** Propagation of the rigid-body fit error into the target $\mathbf{r}$ gives a quadratic form in the residual of $\mathbf{r}$ relative to the centroid:

$$ \text{TRE}^2 = (\Delta \mathbf{r})^{\top} (\Delta \mathbf{r}) $$

**Step 3.** For an isotropic FLE and large $N$, the principal axes of the fiducial configuration define principal lengths $f_1, f_2, f_3$, and the target's projection distances onto these axes are $d_1, d_2, d_3$.

**Step 4.** Averaging over the noise ensemble:

$$ \langle \text{TRE}^2 \rangle = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{N}\left(1 + \dfrac{d_1^2}{3f_1^2} + \dfrac{d_2^2}{3f_2^2} + \dfrac{d_3^2}{3f_3^2}\right) $$

**Engineering takeaway:** To *halve* your surgical error, either **double $N$** or **spread fiducials** so that $f_k^2 \gg d_k^2$. This is why surgeons prefer *bone-anchored* fiducials around the surgical target rather than skin-mounted ones.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Image-to-Tool Transform Chain (Practice Numerical)

A neurosurgical robot performs biopsy. The following calibrated transforms are given (each in homogeneous 4×4 form, omitted here for brevity):

$$ {}^{I}T_{P} = \text{image-to-patient}, \quad {}^{P}T_{R} = \text{patient-to-tracker}, \quad {}^{R}T_{T} = \text{tracker-to-tool tip} $$

**Find the image coordinates of the tool tip.**

**Step 1.** Compose the chain:

$$ {}^{I}T_{T} = {}^{I}T_{P} \cdot {}^{P}T_{R} \cdot {}^{R}T_{T} $$

**Step 2.** Multiply left-to-right (post-multiplication convention). The result is a 4×4 matrix:

$$ {}^{I}T_{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R}_{4\times 4} & \mathbf{t}_{3\times 1} \\ \mathbf{0}_{1\times 3} & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$

**Step 3.** The 3×1 column $\mathbf{t}$ gives the tool-tip coordinates in image (voxel) space. This is the single value the surgeon's planning software displays as *"Tool Tip = (x, y, z) in pre-op MRI."*

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Stewart Platform Inverse Kinematics (Hexapod Robots)

A Stewart platform (used in neurosurgical robots like NeuroMate) has 6 legs of variable length $L_i$ connecting a fixed base to a moving platform. Given desired platform pose $(\mathbf{t}, \mathbf{R})$, find the leg lengths.

**Step 1.** Base anchor $i$ position in base frame: $\mathbf{b}_i$.
**Step 2.** Platform anchor $i$ position in base frame: $\mathbf{p}_i = \mathbf{t} + \mathbf{R}\mathbf{a}_i$, where $\mathbf{a}_i$ is the anchor in the platform frame.
**Step 3.** Leg length is the norm of the difference:

$$ L_i = \lVert \mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{b}_i \rVert = \sqrt{(\mathbf{t} + \mathbf{R}\mathbf{a}_i - \mathbf{b}_i)^{\top}(\mathbf{t} + \mathbf{R}\mathbf{a}_i - \mathbf{b}_i)} $$

**Step 4.** Solve for the actuator prismatic displacement:

$$ \Delta L_i = L_i - L_{i,0} $$

This is the **inverse kinematics** of the parallel manipulator. There is *no closed-form forward kinematics* — the forward problem requires solving a 6th-degree polynomial. This is the engineering reason hexapod robots are *stiff* but *computationally expensive* in forward mode.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Numerical Type)

> **Problem:** A pedicle-screw robot uses 4 fiducials. The image-space and patient-space coordinates (in mm) are:
>
> $$ \mathbf{p}_1=(0,0,0),\ \mathbf{p}_2=(50,0,0),\ \mathbf{p}_3=(0,50,0),\ \mathbf{p}_4=(0,0,50) $$
> $$ \mathbf{q}_1=(1,2,3),\ \mathbf{q}_2=(52,1,3),\ \mathbf{q}_3=(2,53,4),\ \mathbf{q}_4=(1,3,54) $$
>
> **Compute the FRE.**

**Step 1.** Compute centroids:
$$ \bar{\mathbf{p}} = (12.5, 12.5, 12.5), \quad \bar{\mathbf{q}} = (14.0, 14.75, 16.0) $$

**Step 2.** For a translation-only system ($\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$, target is centroid-alignment), the optimal translation is:
$$ \mathbf{t}^{*} = \bar{\mathbf{q}} - \bar{\mathbf{p}} = (1.5, 2.25, 3.5) $$

**Step 3.** Compute residuals for each fiducial:
$$ \mathbf{e}_i = \mathbf{p}_i + \mathbf{t}^{*} - \mathbf{q}_i $$

For $i=1$: $(0+1.5-1, 0+2.25-2, 0+3.5-3) = (0.5, 0.25, 0.5)$, $\lVert \mathbf{e}_1 \rVert^2 = 0.5625$

For $i=2$: $(50+1.5-52, 0+2.25-1, 0+3.5-3) = (-0.5, 1.25, 0.5)$, $\lVert \mathbf{e}_2 \rVert^2 = 2.0625$

For $i=3$: $(0+1.5-2, 50+2.25-53, 0+3.5-4) = (-0.5, -0.75, -0.5)$, $\lVert \mathbf{e}_3 \rVert^2 = 1.1875$

For $i=4$: $(0+1.5-1, 0+2.25-3, 50+3.5-54) = (0.5, -0.75, -0.5)$, $\lVert \mathbf{e}_4 \rVert^2 = 1.1875$

**Step 4.** Mean squared residual:
$$ \dfrac{1}{4}(0.5625 + 2.0625 + 1.1875 + 1.1875) = \dfrac{5.0}{4} = 1.25 $$

**Step 5.** FRE:
$$ \boxed{\text{FRE} = \sqrt{1.25} \approx 1.118 \text{ mm} } $$

This is a **board-typical 7-mark numerical**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Robotic Neurosurgical System (NeuroArm Class)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IMG["PRE-OPERATIVE IMAGING LAYER"]
        MRI1[MRI Scanner 3T]
        CT1[CT Scanner Slice 0.5 mm]
        MRI1 --> VOL1[DICOM Volume Stack]
        CT1 --> VOL1
        VOL1 --> SEG1[Multi-modal Segmentation]
        SEG1 --> PLAN1[Surgeon Planning Station]
    end

    subgraph OR["INTRA-OPERATIVE LAYER"]
        PAT1[Patient Skull]
        FRAME1[Stereotactic Frame / Optical Tracker]
        PAT1 --> FRAME1
        FRAME1 --> REG1[Patient Tracker Polhemus]
        REG1 --> CALC1[Registration Engine Arun SVD]
        PLAN1 --> CALC1
        CALC1 --> FREcalc[FRE Computation]
        FREcalc --> TREcalc[TRE Prediction Fitzpatrick]
    end

    subgraph ROB["ROBOTIC MANIPULATOR LAYER"]
        TREcalc --> CTRL1[Motion Controller]
        CTRL1 --> AC1[Active Constraint Engine]
        AC1 --> HAPTIC1[Haptic Device Surgeon Side]
        HAPTIC1 --> CTRL1
        CTRL1 --> KIN1[Inverse Kinematics Stewart]
        KIN1 --> ACT1[6 Actuator Prismatic Legs]
        ACT1 --> PLAT1[Moving Platform End Effector]
        PLAT1 --> TOOL1[Biopsy Needle or Electrode]
        TOOL1 --> PAT1
    end

    subgraph FB["FEEDBACK & SAFETY LAYER"]
        PLAT1 --> ENC1[Encoder Readback]
        ENC1 --> CTRL1
        TOOL1 --> FTS1[Force Torque Sensor]
        FTS1 --> AC1
        AC1 --> SAFE1[Safety Watchdog Virtual Fixtures]
        SAFE1 --> CTRL1
    end
```

> **Reading guide:** The four layered blocks map directly to the KTU syllabus — *Imaging → Registration → Manipulation → Feedback*. The `Active Constraint Engine` enforces the no-go volume; `Encoder Readback` closes the position loop; `Force Torque Sensor` closes the force loop. This is the canonical closed-loop CIS (Computer-Integrated Surgery) architecture.

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Robotic Orthopaedic System (Mako Class)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRE["PRE-OPERATIVE"]
        CTscan[CT Scan of Joint]
        CTscan --> Plan3D[3D Implant Planning Software]
        Plan3D --> HapticBone[Virtual Bone Model Haptic]
    end

    subgraph INTRA["INTRA-OPERATIVE"]
        BoneCheck[Bone Array Checkpoint Pins]
        BoneCheck --> TrackerOpt[Optical Tracker NDI Polaris]
        TrackerOpt --> RegMako[Surface Match Registration]
        Plan3D --> RegMako
        RegMako --> CorMako[Registration Correlator]
        CorMako --> TREGUI[TRE Display Surgeon GUI]
    end

    subgraph HAPTIC["HAPTIC ARM"]
        SurgeonHand[Surgeon Hand]
        SurgeonHand --> TactileMod[Haptic Stylus Tactile]
        TactileMod --> VirtWall[Virtual Wall Bone Boundaries]
        VirtWall --> BoneMod[3D Virtual Bone Haptic Render]
        BoneMod --> SurgeonHand
    end

    subgraph CUT["ROBOTIC CUTTING ARM"]
        SurgeonHand --> Constrain[Constrained Robotic Arm]
        Constrain --> Burr1[High Speed Burr]
        Burr1 --> BoneCut[Patient Bone Resection]
        BoneMod --> Constrain
    end

    subgraph FB2["FEEDBACK"]
        BoneCut --> BoneMod
        Constrain --> EncoderArm[Encoder Position]
        EncoderArm --> VirtWall
    end
```

> **Reading guide:** Unlike the neurosurgical robot (which *holds* a tool near a deformable target), the orthopaedic robot (Mako-class) **constrains** a hand-held high-speed burr. The surgeon provides the gross motion; the robot provides the haptic boundary. The "Virtual Wall" is the haptic realization of the planned bone-cut envelope.

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture — Parallel vs Serial Manipulator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PAR["PARALLEL HEXAPOD - NEUROSURGERY"]
        BASE1[Fixed Base]
        BASE1 --> LEG1[Prismatic Leg 1]
        BASE1 --> LEG2[Prismatic Leg 2]
        BASE1 --> LEG3[Prismatic Leg 3]
        BASE1 --> LEG4[Prismatic Leg 4]
        BASE1 --> LEG5[Prismatic Leg 5]
        BASE1 --> LEG6[Prismatic Leg 6]
        LEG1 --> PLAT2[Moving Platform Tool Tip]
        LEG2 --> PLAT2
        LEG3 --> PLAT2
        LEG4 --> PLAT2
        LEG5 --> PLAT2
        LEG6 --> PLAT2
    end

    subgraph SER["SERIAL 6-DOF - ORTHOPAEDIC"]
        PED[Pedestal Base]
        PED --> J1[Revolute Joint 1 Shoulder]
        J1 --> L1[Link Upper Arm]
        L1 --> J2[Revolute Joint 2 Elbow]
        J2 --> L2[Link Forearm]
        L2 --> J3[Revolute Joint 3]
        J3 --> L3[Wrist Link]
        L3 --> J4[Revolute Joint 4]
        J4 --> L4[Tool Flange]
        L4 --> J5[Revolute Joint 5]
        J5 --> L6[End Effector Burr]
    end
```

> **Reading guide:** Parallel hexapods give high stiffness and low inertia (good for steady stereotactic tool-holding). Serial arms give larger dexterous workspace (good for cutting across an entire knee joint). The KTU board frequently tests this trade-off.

## 4.4 Image Registration Pipeline (Common to Both)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Pre-op CT MRI] --> B[Segmentation Target + Fiducials]
    B --> C[Define Surgical Plan Trajectory]
    D[Intra-op Patient] --> E[Acquire Fiducial Points]
    E --> F[Pair Corresponding Points]
    C --> F
    F --> G[Compute Rigid Transform Arun SVD]
    G --> H[FRE Metric]
    H --> I{TRE less than threshold?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Proceed to Surgery]
    I -->|No| K[Re-acquire Fiducials]
    K --> E
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between active and passive surgical robots. Give one example of each in orthopaedic surgery.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> An **active surgical robot** performs a pre-programmed surgical task autonomously (under surgeon supervision), e.g., **ROBODOC** which mills the femoral canal for hip implant insertion.
>
> A **passive (or semi-active) surgical robot** provides geometric guidance or constraints but the surgeon retains control of the actual cutting action, e.g., **Mako** system which constrains the surgeon's hand-held burr within a haptic virtual boundary.
>
> | Feature | Active | Passive / Semi-Active |
> |---|---|---|
> | Action | Autonomous cutting | Surgeon-guided |
> | Safety | Watchdog required | Inherently safe |
> | Example | ROBODOC, CASPAR | Mako, NAVIO |
> | Regulatory Class | Higher | Lower |

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is brain-shift, and why is it a critical challenge for robotic neurosurgery?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> Brain-shift is the intra-operative displacement of brain parenchyma (typically 5–20 mm) due to gravity, CSF drainage, tumour debulking, and anaesthesia-induced swelling. It is critical because:
>
> 1. **It invalidates pre-operative MRI registration** — the pre-op image no longer matches the live anatomy.
> 2. It causes **targeting errors** for biopsy, electrode placement (DBS), and tumour resection.
> 3. Mitigation methods include **intra-operative MRI/CT**, **deformable registration algorithms**, and **real-time ultrasound fusion**. (3 marks distributed: 1 mark definition, 1 mark why critical, 1 mark mitigation)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a robotic neurosurgical system. List the major functional subsystems and explain the role of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the image-to-patient registration using the Arun-Huang SVD method. Show all key steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Architecture (7 marks)

A robotic neurosurgical system is composed of **four tightly coupled functional subsystems**:

1. **Imaging Subsystem (1.5 marks)** — Pre-operative CT/MRI acquisition, intra-operative fluoroscopy or iMRI. Outputs DICOM volumes; resolution ≤ 1 mm voxel.

2. **Localization & Registration Subsystem (2 marks)** — Optical (NDI Polaris) or electromagnetic trackers; fiducial arrays screwed into the skull; Arun-Huang SVD solver computes ${}^{I}T_{P}$; outputs FRE/TRE.

3. **Mechanical Manipulator Subsystem (1.5 marks)** — Usually a 6-DOF serial or Stewart parallel platform. End-effector holds the surgical tool (biopsy needle, electrode, endoscope). Workspace 200 × 200 × 200 mm typical.

4. **Control & HMI Subsystem (1 mark)** — Active-constraint engine enforces no-go zones; haptic device for surgeon feedback; safety watchdog; GUI for trajectory display.

5. **Conclusion (1 mark)** — The closed loop is: image → plan → register → control → sense → update. This is the **Computer-Integrated Surgery (CIS) paradigm**.

#### Model Solution — (b) Derivation (7 marks)

(Present the full derivation from Section 3.1, with the 6 steps. Mark split: 1 mark for problem statement, 2 marks for centroid elimination, 1 mark for SVD formulation, 1 mark for $\mathbf{R}^*$ extraction, 1 mark for $\mathbf{t}^*$ back-substitution, 1 mark for final boxed answer with engineering interpretation.)

> **Final boxed answer:**
> $$ \mathbf{R}^{*} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{U}^{\top}, \quad \mathbf{t}^{*} = \bar{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{R}^{*}\bar{\mathbf{p}} $$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of operation of the Mako robotic system used in total knee arthroplasty. Discuss the role of haptic feedback and CT-based pre-operative planning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define Fiducial Registration Error (FRE) and Target Registration Error (TRE). For a 4-fiducial configuration with FLE = 0.5 mm, principal fiducial spreads $f_1 = 40$ mm, $f_2 = 35$ mm, $f_3 = 30$ mm, and target projection distances $d_1 = 5$ mm, $d_2 = 4$ mm, $d_3 = 3$ mm, compute the mean TRE. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Mako TKA (7 marks)

1. **CT-based planning (2 marks)** — A pre-op CT of the knee is segmented. The surgeon virtually places the implant components (femoral, tibial) and tunes the alignment to the patient's anatomy. The bone-cut envelope is computed as a CAD surface.

2. **Intra-operative registration (2 marks)** — Bone-anchored checkpoint arrays are fixed to femur and tibia. Optical tracker reads their pose. A surface-match algorithm aligns the live bone to the CT bone. FRE displayed to the surgeon (must be ≤ 0.5 mm).

3. **Haptic constraint (2 marks)** — The surgeon holds a hand-piece with a high-speed burr. The robot arm is *back-driveable*; it constrains the burr to stay inside the virtual cut envelope. Forces are rendered to the surgeon's hand via impedance control, providing a "tactile wall" sensation.

4. **Closed-loop milling (1 mark)** — The burr removes bone only within the planned volume. The robot updates the haptic boundary in real time as bone is removed. Implant is cemented and closed.

#### Model Solution — (b) FRE, TRE, Numerical (7 marks)

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **FRE** = root-mean-square distance between corresponding fiducials after registration. Measures *fit quality*.
- **TRE** = distance between the registered target position and its true position. Measures *clinical error* at the target.

**Numerical (5 marks):**

Step 1 — Identify the Fitzpatrick mean TRE² formula:

$$ \langle \text{TRE}^2 \rangle = \dfrac{\text{FLE}^2}{N}\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{3}\sum_{k=1}^{3}\dfrac{d_k^2}{f_k^2}\right) $$

Step 2 — Substitute $N=4$, $\text{FLE} = 0.5$ mm:

$$ \langle \text{TRE}^2 \rangle = \dfrac{0.25}{4}\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{3}\left(\dfrac{5^2}{40^2} + \dfrac{4^2}{35^2} + \dfrac{3^2}{30^2}\right)\right) $$

Step 3 — Compute each term:

$$ \dfrac{5^2}{40^2} = \dfrac{25}{1600} = 0.015625 $$

$$ \dfrac{4^2}{35^2} = \dfrac{16}{1225} \approx 0.013061 $$

$$ \dfrac{3^2}{30^2} = \dfrac{9}{900} = 0.010000 $$

Step 4 — Sum:

$$ 0.015625 + 0.013061 + 0.010000 = 0.038686 $$

Step 5 — Apply the factor 1/3:

$$ \dfrac{0.038686}{3} \approx 0.012895 $$

Step 6 — Add 1:

$$ 1 + 0.012895 = 1.012895 $$

Step 7 — Multiply by FLE²/N:

$$ \langle \text{TRE}^2 \rangle = 0.0625 \times 1.012895 \approx 0.063306 \text{ mm}^2 $$

Step 8 — Take square root:

$$ \boxed{\text{TRE}_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{0.063306} \approx 0.252 \text{ mm} } $$

**Engineering interpretation (½ mark):** The sub-millimetric TRE confirms clinical acceptability of this fiducial configuration. The dominant term is the "1" inside the parentheses; the geometric term contributes only 1.3 % — meaning that *spreading* the fiducials further would not significantly improve accuracy here. The limiting factor is the FLE itself, and improving the localization hardware (e.g., sub-pixel CT) would yield the biggest gain.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing FRE with TRE** — FRE is a *fit metric*, TRE is a *clinical* metric. A low FRE does NOT guarantee a low TRE. (1–2 mark penalty)
> 2. **Forgetting the brain-shift caveat** — Whenever discussing neurosurgical robot accuracy, *always* mention brain-shift and how your system handles it. Examiners reward this. (−1 mark if omitted)
> 3. **Mako is NOT active, it is semi-active** — A surprising number of students write "Mako autonomously cuts bone." It does not. The surgeon drives the burr; the robot constrains it. (−2 marks)
> 4. **Sign convention in SVD** — Many students write $\mathbf{R}^* = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$ instead of $\mathbf{V}\mathbf{U}^{\top}$. The latter is correct when minimizing *image-to-patient*; the former is its transpose. Always recheck $\det(\mathbf{R}^*) = +1$. (−1 mark)
> 5. **Units of FLE / TRE** — Always quote in **mm**; examiners are strict.
> 6. **Skipping derivation steps in Part B** — Even a perfectly correct final answer loses 2–3 marks if intermediate algebra is not shown.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Memorize Before Exam):**

- **Definitions:** Robotic neurosurgery (image-guided, soft-tissue, ≤ 2 mm TRE); robotic orthopaedic surgery (bone-anchored, rigid, ≤ 0.5 mm TRE).
- **Historical Firsts:** PUMA 200 (1985, first neurosurgical), ROBODOC (1988, first orthopaedic), NeuroMate (1992, first commercial stereotactic).
- **Registration:** Arun-Huang SVD method — derive $\mathbf{R}^*, \mathbf{t}^*$ analytically.
- **FRE vs TRE:** FRE = fit quality, TRE = clinical error. Fitzpatrick's equation relates both via FLE and fiducial geometry.
- **Fitzpatrick formula:** $\langle \text{TRE}^2 \rangle = (\text{FLE}^2 / N)\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{3}\sum d_k^2/f_k^2\right)$. Spread fiducials far from target.
- **Stewart Platform IK:** $L_i = \lVert \mathbf{t} + \mathbf{R}\mathbf{a}_i - \mathbf{b}_i \rVert$. Forward kinematics has no closed form.
- **Active constraints:** Virtual fixtures — software-defined no-go zones, enforced via impedance/admittance control.
- **Mako = semi-active, NOT active**; uses haptic constraint on surgeon-held burr.
- **Brain-shift:** 5–20 mm; mitigated by iMRI, ultrasound fusion, deformable registration.
- **da Vinci:** Teleoperated, NOT autonomous; surgeon sits at console.
- **Key constants:** Accuracy (ortho ≤ 0.5 mm, neuro ≤ 2 mm); workspace ~ 200×200×200 mm³; haptic cutoff ~ 8 Hz tremor filter.
- **Formula Sheet:** Master the 10-row cheat table in Section 2.3.
- **Safety:** Always quote a watchdog / fail-stop architecture in any robotic surgical design answer.
